title: Reliability of asymptotic work extraction
authors: Kaito Watanabe, Bartosz Regula, Marco Tomamichel, Ryuji Takagi
abstract: Extracting work from quantum states is a fundamental task in quantum thermodynamics. Previous studies have primarily focused on determining the best achievable rate of work extraction, and remarkably, this characterization appeared to remain unchanged regardless of the choice of allowed processes: whether one considers the operationally motivated class of energy-conserving thermal operations, or the axiomatic class of Gibbs-preserving operations, the optimal extractable work is given by the Helmholtz free energy. Here, we challenge this perspective, showing that a more refined analysis of the asymptotic performance of work extraction reveals significant differences in the performance for the two different classes of free operations. Precisely, we focus on the trade-off between the extraction rate and its reliability, characterized by the optimal asymptotic speed at which the extraction error can be suppressed. We establish that the reliability of Gibbs-preserving operations and of thermal operations are respectively characterized by the Petz and the sandwiched Rényi relative entropies, demonstrating that the former in general strictly outperforms the latter, and providing new interpretations of several information-theoretic divergences. Our analysis reveals that operational constraints such as energy conservation impose stronger limitations on the achievable precision of quantum tasks than can be inferred from their asymptotic rates, thereby questioning the use of Gibbs-preserving operations as a mathematically convenient substitute for the physically realizable thermal processes.
summary: This work rigorously compares two models for free operations—Gibbs-preserving and thermal—in the context of quantum work extraction. It demonstrates that while both yield the same asymptotic work rate, their reliability (error exponent) differs significantly. This reveals a fundamental operational gap, suggesting that physically constrained processes are more limited than mathematically convenient ones.
main_problem: The paper challenges the assumption that the optimal asymptotic work extraction rate is universally characterized by the Helmholtz free energy when comparing different classes of allowed quantum operations.
main_result: A significant operational gap exists between Gibbs-preserving operations and physically realizable thermal operations, particularly concerning the reliability (error exponent) of the extraction process in the zero-rate limit.
method: The analysis uses resource theory concepts applied to work extraction, comparing performance metrics derived from quantum hypothesis testing divergences (Petz vs. sandwiched Rényi entropies).
model_or_system: The study models work extraction from a general quantum state ($ho$) coupled to an auxiliary 'work battery' ($X_m$), analyzed under two classes of free operations: Gibbs-preserving operations (GPO) and physically motivated thermal operations (TO).
key_observables: Optimal asymptotic error exponents ($B_{GPO,ext}, B_{TO,ext}$) and the zero-rate error exponent ($	ext{BZ.R.}$), quantified by various quantum relative entropies.
important_parameters: The inverse temperature $ eta$, the rate of work extraction $r$, and the coherence/incoherence condition $[ho, 	au]$.
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on comparing asymptotic limits derived from generalized Rényi divergences and assumes specific covariance conditions for certain data-processing inequalities to hold.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the strict separation of reliability functions ($	ext{H}_r > e^{	ext{H}_r}$) when quantum states exhibit coherence, quantifying the operational gap between GPO and TO.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by first establishing the equivalence of work definitions at the asymptotic rate (first order), then rigorously comparing error exponents in fixed-rate regimes, and finally focusing on the zero-rate limit to quantify the fundamental difference between operation classes.
why_interesting: It provides a deep theoretical understanding of operational constraints in quantum thermodynamics, showing that mathematical convenience (GPO) can mask physical limitations (TO) when precision is required.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
